findings.summary: agents/cli/handlers.py contains 15 pass-through functions that do nothing but forward a fixed (client_class, client_name) pair to a shared generic implementation, providing no behavioral differentiation
evidence: handle_jules_execute(args) -> _handle_agent_execute(JulesClient, 'Jules', args) — one-liner, zero logic
handle_jules_stream(args) -> _handle_agent_stream(JulesClient, 'Jules', args) — one-liner, zero logic
handle_jules_check(args) -> _handle_cli_agent_check(JulesClient, 'Jules', args) — one-liner
Same pattern repeated for Claude, Codex, OpenCode: 15 wrappers confirmed by static analysis (pass_through_wrappers count=15 for this file)

The generic _handle_agent_execute / _handle_agent_stream already accept client_class and client_name as parameters, so the per-agent shims add no indirection value — a dispatch table or partial application would eliminate the repetition
findings.suggestion: Replace the per-agent one-liner shims with a data-driven dispatch table: AGENT_REGISTRY = {'jules': (JulesClient, 'Jules', 'cli'), ...} and route CLI commands through a single parameterised handler. This collapses 15 functions to 0 and makes adding a new agent a one-line registry entry.

findings.summary: Two parallel and structurally incompatible BarChart implementations exist in data_visualization: one in charts/bar_chart.py (matplotlib-direct, function-first) and one in plots/bar_chart.py (BasePlot subclass, backend-agnostic). Both are exported and both are imported from plotter.py, creating redundant abstraction with no clear ownership rule.
evidence: data_visualization/charts/bar_chart.py defines create_bar_chart() function + BarChart wrapper class using matplotlib directly via plt.subplots
data_visualization/plots/bar_chart.py defines a different BarChart class that extends BasePlot and uses FigureCanvasAgg via to_html()
data_visualization/engines/plotter.py imports from charts/ only (create_bar_chart, create_line_plot etc.), while plots/_base.py is imported by 20 files

The two BarChart classes have incompatible constructors: charts version takes (categories, values, ...) positional; plots version stores via (title, data, categories, values, **kwargs) mixed interface
No documented rule establishes when to use charts/ vs plots/ vs engines/; all three live under data_visualization/
findings.suggestion: Decide on one canonical implementation. The plots/ hierarchy built on BasePlot is more principled (backend-agnostic, testable without pyplot). Deprecate and remove the charts/ functional duplicates, migrating Plotter to wrap BasePlot subclasses directly. If the matplotlib-direct path must remain for performance, document the explicit boundary and remove BarChart from charts/ to avoid the class name collision.

findings.summary: advanced_plotter.py accumulates 9 wide-parameter functions and 50 config_bag mentions, driven by an over-broad PlotConfig dataclass that bundles unrelated presentation, persistence, and display concerns into a single object passed through every call site.
evidence: PlotConfig contains 13 fields spanning rendering (figsize, dpi, style, palette, grid, legend), persistence (save_format, save_dpi, bbox_inches), and interactivity (show_plot, transparent, tight_layout)
Static analysis reports 9 wide-function signatures and 50 config_bag_mentions in this single file
plot_line() takes 9 explicit parameters (label, color, linewidth, linestyle, marker, markersize, alpha, **kwargs) on top of self.config, creating two implicit parameter bags simultaneously
PlotConfig.show_plot bool controls side-effectful display behaviour from within a dataclass — mixing value-object semantics with action semantics
findings.suggestion: Split PlotConfig into at least two focused objects: RenderOptions (figsize, dpi, style, palette, grid, legend) and SaveOptions (save_format, save_dpi, bbox_inches, transparent). Remove show_plot from the config entirely — callers should explicitly call fig.show() or plt.show() after the fact. This eliminates the implicit side-effect from the config bag.

findings.summary: logging_monitoring imports from model_context_protocol (decorators.py) while model_context_protocol imports from logging_monitoring, creating a circular dependency between two declared Foundation Layer modules.
evidence: logging_monitoring/mcp_tools.py line 10: `from codomyrmex.model_context_protocol.decorators import mcp_tool`
model_context_protocol/decorators.py line 14: `from codomyrmex.logging_monitoring import get_logger`
Both modules are documented as Foundation Layer in CLAUDE.md: 'logging_monitoring' and 'model_context_protocol' are listed as Foundation Layer peers
The cycle is real at import time if mcp_tools.py is loaded during model_context_protocol initialisation — Python's import machinery will produce a partial module object for one of them
findings.suggestion: Break the cycle by having logging_monitoring/mcp_tools.py perform a lazy import of mcp_tool inside the decorated functions rather than at module level. Alternatively, move the @mcp_tool application for logging_monitoring tools into a separate adapter module outside the foundation layer (e.g., logging_monitoring/mcp_adapter.py imported only by the bridge scanner, not at module init).

findings.summary: utils/process/subprocess.py imports from subprocess_advanced.py at module bottom, while subprocess_advanced.py imports internal helpers from subprocess.py, creating a real import cycle that the static analysis detected.
evidence: subprocess.py line 647: `from .subprocess_advanced import stream_command, run_with_retry  # noqa: E402, I001` — placed at bottom-of-file to avoid NameError
subprocess_advanced.py lines 18-24: `from .subprocess import (SubprocessResult, _prepare_command, _prepare_environment, _validate_working_directory, run_command,)`
The bottom-of-file placement with noqa comments is a code smell documenting that the author knew this was a cycle requiring workaround
Static analysis confirms: 'Import cycle (2 files): src/codomyrmex/utils/process/subprocess.py -> src/codomyrmex/utils/process/subprocess_advanced.py'
findings.suggestion: Invert the dependency: subprocess.py should contain only the core primitives (run_command, SubprocessResult, CommandError, _prepare_* helpers). subprocess_advanced.py imports from subprocess.py and adds stream_command and run_with_retry. The re-export of advanced functions from subprocess.py should be removed — callers that need streaming should import directly from subprocess_advanced. This eliminates the cycle without workarounds.

findings.summary: data_visualization exposes three parallel chart implementation paths (charts/, plots/, engines/) imported by different callers with no declared architectural boundary, causing uncontrolled coupling across internal subsystems.
evidence: plots/_base.py has 20 importers — highest in the codebase — yet lives alongside charts/ which provides duplicate chart types with overlapping names (both directories contain bar_chart.py, heatmap.py, histogram.py, line_plot.py, pie_chart.py, scatter_plot.py)
engines/plotter.py imports exclusively from charts/ but engines/advanced_plotter.py is a standalone 1000+ LOC class with no relation to charts/ or plots/
Three separate utils surfaces: data_visualization/utils.py, engines/plot_utils (referenced in utils.py comment), and _compat.py — three separate utility modules inside one module
No __init__.py or README distinguishes which path (charts/, plots/, engines/) is the public API vs internal implementation
findings.suggestion: Define a single public API surface in data_visualization/__init__.py. Designate plots/ as the canonical object hierarchy (BasePlot → subclasses) and charts/ as the functional convenience layer wrapping it. Consolidate utils.py and _compat.py into one _internal.py. The engines/ subdirectory should contain only the AdvancedPlotter (high-power matplotlib surface) with a clear note that it is opt-in and not the default path.

findings.summary: numpy, scipy, networkx, and fastapi are declared as unconditional core dependencies, pulling heavy scientific and web-framework stacks into every installation regardless of which modules are used.
evidence: pyproject.toml [project.dependencies] line 49: `numpy>=2.2.0` — unconditional core dependency
pyproject.toml line 76: `scipy>=1.7.0` — comment says 'used in cerebrum'; cerebrum is a non-foundation module
pyproject.toml line 74: `networkx>=3.0.0` — comment says 'used in cerebrum'
pyproject.toml lines 58-59: `fastapi>=0.100.0`, `uvicorn>=0.23.0` — full ASGI web framework as core dep
pyproject.toml line 43: `pylint>=3.0.0`, `bandit>=1.8.0`, `radon>=6.0.0`, `lizard>=1.17.0` — static analysis tools as core runtime deps (not dev group)
The optional-dependencies section exists and is used for cloud, serialization, etc. but the most heavyweight deps (numpy, scipy, fastapi, static analysis tools) bypassed it
findings.suggestion: Move numpy, scipy, networkx to a [visualization] or [science] optional extra. Move fastapi, uvicorn to an [api] optional extra. Move pylint, bandit, radon, lizard to the [dev] dependency group (they are tooling, not runtime). Audit whether mnemonic (BIP-39 wallet) and msgpack (serialization) belong in core. Reducing core deps significantly improves cold install time and avoids version conflicts for users who only need a subset of modules.

findings.summary: Three confirmed import cycles exist in the codebase, each representing a structural coupling defect. While the subprocess and api cycles are minor, they indicate the dependency graph is not being actively monitored.
evidence: Static analysis confirmed: 'Import cycle (2 files): src/codomyrmex/dependency_injection/container.py -> src/codomyrmex/dependency_injection/scopes.py'
Static analysis confirmed: 'Import cycle (2 files): src/codomyrmex/utils/process/subprocess.py -> src/codomyrmex/utils/process/subprocess_advanced.py'
Static analysis confirmed: 'Import cycle (2 files): src/codomyrmex/api/openapi_generator.py -> src/codomyrmex/api/openapi_standardization_generator.py'

dependency_injection/scopes.py uses TYPE_CHECKING guard for container.py import (line 31: `if TYPE_CHECKING: from .container import Container`) which is the correct pattern — but the cycle is still detected, suggesting at least one non-TYPE_CHECKING path exists
No CI check for import cycles is evident; cycles will accumulate silently
findings.suggestion: Add `python -c 'import codomyrmex'` and a cycle-detection lint step (e.g., pydeps or import-linter) to CI to prevent future cycle introduction. For the api/ cycle, extract the shared dataclasses (APISchema, OpenAPISpecification) into a separate api/models.py that both generators import without mutual dependency.

findings.summary: data_visualization defines three independent base/abstract classes (BasePlot, BaseComponent, BaseReport) using unrelated rendering contracts, with no common protocol or interface connecting them, yet all three are high-fan-in shared surfaces.
evidence: BasePlot.render() returns a placeholder div; BasePlot.to_html() returns a base64 PNG img tag — two methods on the same class with different rendering contracts (placeholder vs real)
BaseComponent.render() returns a placeholder div (same string as BasePlot.render()) with no actual content — the default implementation is indistinguishable from BasePlot's placeholder
BaseReport.render() returns an article tag containing sections, using a lambda trick `getattr(s, 'render', lambda s=s: str(s))` which is an overly defensive pattern for a private method
All three define `render() -> str` with incompatible semantics and no shared Protocol or ABC enforcing the contract
data_visualization/reports/_base.py has a 24-line body and 6 importers — it provides almost no behaviour while requiring 6 modules to take a dependency on it
findings.suggestion: Define a single Renderable Protocol with render() -> str. All three base classes should implement it. Remove the duplicate placeholder render() from BasePlot (it contradicts to_html() which is the actual renderer). Consolidate BaseReport's defensive lambda with a direct isinstance check or make sections typed to Renderable. This eliminates the three-way fragmentation and makes the rendering contract explicit.

findings.summary: exceptions/base.py defines CodomyrmexError as the project-wide root exception (12 importers, highest fan-in outside data_visualization) but the Foundation Layer modules that should enforce it (logging_monitoring, environment_setup) do not import it, allowing inconsistent exception types at the lowest level.
evidence: exceptions/base.py has 12 importers (second highest fan-in in the codebase) — it is clearly intended as a shared root
grep of logging_monitoring/ and environment_setup/ for 'from codomyrmex.exceptions' returns 0 results — the foundation modules do not use the project's own exception hierarchy
logging_monitoring/mcp_tools.py raises bare RuntimeError and ValueError rather than CodomyrmexError subclasses

110 modules across the codebase import from exceptions — demonstrating broad adoption — while the foundation layer is excluded, creating an incoherence where the bottom of the stack is exempt from the error contract it supposedly provides to everyone above it
findings.suggestion: Either: (a) move CodomyrmexError to a truly zero-dependency location (e.g., src/codomyrmex/_base_errors.py imported by logging_monitoring and exceptions alike), or (b) accept that logging_monitoring is the only foundation module that is exceptions-exempt and document this explicitly. Either way, logging_monitoring should raise CodomyrmexError subclasses from its MCP tools so that callers get consistent error types.

findings.summary: Multiple log files, data files, and processing artifacts are present at the repository root alongside source code, indicating the project root is being used as a working directory for operational output rather than a clean source boundary.
evidence: Git status shows untracked root-level files: all_models.txt, list_all_models.py, list_models.py, patch_basic_usage.py, patch_docs.py
Repo root contains codomyrmex.log, basic_image.log, basic_image_venv.log, basic_video.log, basic_video2.log, basic_video3.log (visible in ls output)
patch_basic_usage.py and patch_docs.py are one-off maintenance scripts left at root rather than placed in scripts/maintenance/

list_all_models.py and list_models.py are utility scripts that belong in scripts/maintenance/ alongside the existing list_all_models.py and list_models.py already there — creating duplicate files at different paths
findings.suggestion: Add *.log to .gitignore if not already present. Move patch_basic_usage.py, patch_docs.py, list_all_models.py, list_models.py to scripts/maintenance/ (deduplicating against existing files there). Add an @output/ or outputs/ gitignore rule to prevent operational artifacts from appearing in git status. The repo root should contain only configuration, documentation, and entry-point files.

findings.summary: data_visualization contains three directories (charts/, plots/, engines/) that all implement chart types with overlapping names and no documented placement rule, making it impossible for a new contributor to determine where a new chart type belongs.
evidence: charts/ contains: area_chart.py, bar_chart.py, box_plot.py, heatmap.py, histogram.py, line_plot.py, pie_chart.py, scatter_plot.py

plots/ contains: area.py, bar_chart.py, box.py, candlestick.py, confusion_matrix.py, funnel.py, gantt.py, heatmap.py, histogram.py, line_plot.py, mermaid.py, network.py, pie.py, radar.py, sankey.py, scatter.py, treemap.py, violin.py, wordcloud.py
bar_chart.py, heatmap.py, histogram.py, line_plot.py, scatter_plot.py/scatter.py exist in both charts/ and plots/ with different implementations and different class names for the same chart type
engines/ contains advanced_plotter.py (1000+ LOC standalone class) and plotter.py (importer-facade over charts/) — two different architectural patterns side-by-side
plots/_base.py has 20 importers but charts/bar_chart.py is what Plotter uses — the most-imported file is not the one the primary entry point uses
findings.suggestion: Collapse charts/ and plots/ into a single charts/ directory using the plots/ implementations (more complete, backend-agnostic via BasePlot). Rename charts/ files to match plots/ naming (scatter.py not scatter_plot.py). Make engines/ the home only for matplotlib-heavy classes (AdvancedPlotter). This eliminates the three-way directory confusion and gives a clear answer to 'where does a new chart type go?'
